lens.included_activities: Independent US steel investment foundries that repeatedly produce detailed lost-wax steel castings to external aerospace, defense, turbine, industrial, medical, automotive, fluid-handling, or other customer specifications and have transferable operations in the target band.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive OEM foundries, non-steel investment casters, conventional steel or iron foundries, finished-product manufacturers classified by their final product, project shells, and non-control interests.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is earned through tooling, qualification, casting, heat-treatment, finishing, inspection, and sometimes machining charges under repeat part programs and purchase orders, commonly with alloy and energy surcharges.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code includes externally contracted and captive casting operations. The lens retains independent repeat casting programs because captive production is not an outsourced service.

a.bridge: BLS measures all foundries at NAICS 331500 rather than steel investment foundries. I assign AI exposure to RFQ and drawing review, manufacturability analysis, tooling documentation, process planning, scheduling, quality and certification records, maintenance knowledge, and administration, and low substitution to wax injection, tree assembly, repeated shell coating, dewaxing, firing, melting, pouring, knockout, finishing, and inspection. Investment foundries likely have more engineering and quality work than the aggregate, so the proxy probably understates exposure.
a.rationale: All foundries are 66.92% production employment and 7.33% installation, maintenance, and repair, constraining direct AI substitution. Investment casting's detailed, consistent parts and one-use wax-and-ceramic molds add engineering, planning, documentation, traceability, and defect-analysis work, raising exposed share relative to commodity sand casting while leaving the process physically intensive.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census across aerospace, turbine, defense, automotive, and industrial steel investment foundries, including current simulation, automation, and quality systems, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Four-digit occupation data include much less engineering-intensive casting methods.
Existing simulation, robotic dipping, automated inspection, and digital quality systems are not separately observed.

rho.rationale: AI-assisted quote and drawing review, process-plan drafting, tooling knowledge, scheduling, defect analysis, maintenance retrieval, certificates, and customer communication can be implemented within five years. Aerospace and defense validation, export controls, customer approvals, traceability, cybersecurity, small failure samples, and accountable engineering and quality release limit realization. Direct autonomous process changes are excluded.
rho.change_evidence: Validated pilots measuring quote cycle, engineering hours, first-pass yield, shell and casting scrap, rework, schedule attainment, certification errors, and customer escapes would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No six-digit longitudinal AI adoption study was located.
High-specification plants may have larger document opportunity but slower validation and change control.

e.rationale: Customer-funded tooling, long-lived qualified parts, recurring purchase orders, and complete-to-print casting programs fit the outsourced-service lens well. Eligibility is reduced by captive plants, customer concentration, firms classified by finished product, environmental liabilities, target-band economics, and owner-dependent technical relationships. The injected n remains a size-class and margin estimate.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level map of external recurring-program revenue, captive status, certifications, customer concentration, normalized EBITDA, tooling ownership, permits, and standalone management would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Public data do not quantify captive versus independent foundries.
Aerospace-program revenue can be recurring yet highly concentrated and vulnerable to platform changes.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures planned sales or transfers among US employer-business owners generally, not completed steel-investment-foundry control transfers. I anchor near its 22% employer intention rate and discount for customer approvals, export controls, technical-owner dependence, permits, and capital needs that can prevent a qualifying close. Intentions likely overstate completions.
s5.rationale: General owner aging supports transfer supply, and qualified tooling, programs, workforce, and process know-how can transfer as a going concern. Highly strategic assets may also sell to larger aerospace or industrial groups, while weak or customer-concentrated plants may close instead.
s5.change_evidence: Owner ages and five years of closed investment-casting deals, closures, and permit ownership changes from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, SBA records, AFS/ICI advisers, and customer approval notices would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Cross-industry intentions are not completed deal probabilities.
Strategic asset acquisitions and internal reorganizations may not qualify as standalone LMM transfers.

q.rationale: Qualification, customer-specific tooling, detailed geometries, alloy/process approvals, and high switching costs can preserve operational gains. Long-term aerospace and industrial customers still demand annual productivity, reopen prices, and dual-source parts; disclosed gains will be shared. High-complexity, low-volume, or capacity-constrained programs retain more than mature high-volume parts.
q.change_evidence: Long-term agreements, annual productivity clauses, sole-source status, tooling ownership, bid history, surcharge formulas, and part-level contribution-margin bridges would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No source directly measures AI-productivity pass-through.
Certification, alloy, energy, and yield costs can mask retained labor benefit.

d5.bridge: BLS projects all-foundry output down 0.8% annually but aerospace products and parts output up 2.0% annually; FAA projects the US commercial aircraft fleet rising over the long term. These are different populations from steel investment castings. I weight the more favorable aerospace/precision end markets more heavily than the aggregate foundry trend, while keeping downside for program timing, substitution, and the fact that some investment castings use non-steel alloys outside this code. The aerospace proxy may overstate firms serving industrial or automotive markets.
d5.rationale: Investment casting serves high-value aerospace, defense, turbines, medical, fluid-handling, oil and gas, automotive, and industrial applications. BLS projects aerospace output from 281.6 to 343.5 billion chained dollars through 2034, and FAA forecasts the commercial aircraft fleet rising from 6,949 in 2025 to 10,677 in 2046. That supports five-year growth despite declining aggregate foundry output.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year backlog and customer forecasts by platform, material and end market, engine build rates, defense procurement, industrial turbine orders, part attrition, and additive-manufacturing qualification would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Aerospace evidence includes many parts and materials outside steel investment castings.
Long qualification cycles can delay demand and make individual programs lumpy.

o.rationale: Detailed steel castings require tooling, wax patterns, ceramic shells, dewaxing, firing, melting, pouring, shell removal, finishing, heat treatment, inspection, and accountable certification. Software can automate design exchange and some process planning but cannot supply the physical qualified part. Downside reflects additive manufacturing, machining-from-solid, or customer insourcing, all still physical operator models.
o.change_evidence: Qualification of additive or direct-manufacturing substitutes, customer insourcing plans, redesign rates, and autonomous distributed foundry systems would materially change the estimate.
caveats: This measures need for an accountable operator, not the number of workers.
Physical-process substitution affects d5 and o at the margin, not pure software self-service.

narrative.executive_view: Steel investment foundries fit repeat outsourced manufacturing better than many primary-metal industries because customer-specific tooling and qualifications sustain long programs. AI opportunity is concentrated in engineering, quality, planning, maintenance, and administration, while a laborious one-use wax-and-shell process remains physical.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate drawing and RFQ review, manufacturability analysis, process plans, tooling knowledge, schedules, defect investigations, maintenance retrieval, certificates, and customer communication. Wax patterns, tree assembly, shell dipping, dewaxing, firing, pouring, finishing, and inspection remain operator-intensive.
narrative.value_capture: Qualification and switching costs support retention, particularly for complex, sole-source, or capacity-constrained parts. Annual productivity clauses and dual-sourcing share savings with sophisticated aerospace and industrial buyers.
narrative.firm_availability: Independent program foundries often fit the lens, but captive assets, customer concentration, export controls, certification, permits, capital backlog, and key-person technical knowledge narrow transferability.
narrative.demand_durability: Aerospace and turbine growth support a favorable base relative to aggregate foundries, with defense, medical, fluid-handling, and industrial uses diversifying demand. Program delays and alternate manufacturing technologies widen the range.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Risks include platform concentration, qualification loss, export-control and cybersecurity obligations, shell and casting yield, energy and alloy costs, air emissions, capital needs, additive-manufacturing substitution, and four-digit occupation proxies.
narrative.principal_driver: Long-lived, qualification-heavy aerospace and industrial part programs create durable outsourced relationships and pricing differentiation.
narrative.principal_weakness: The physical one-use mold process dominates labor, while customer concentration and program approvals can make otherwise attractive firms fragile.

sources.title: Foundries - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Aggregate foundry occupation mix: 66.92% production and 7.33% installation, maintenance, and repair.

sources.title: NAICS Definition: Steel Investment Foundries
sources.supports: Official scope and method: steel investment castings use wax shapes coated in refractory slurry, creating seamless molds and highly detailed, consistent castings.

sources.title: Economic Impact Analysis of Final Iron and Steel Foundries NESHAP
sources.supports: Investment-casting process detail: repeated ceramic coating, pattern burnout, and mold heating to about 1,800°F before casting.

sources.title: Advanced Manufacturing Office Update: Investment Casting Projects
sources.supports: Examples of complete-to-print complex investment castings and finished subassemblies supplied to automotive and aerospace customers.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Cross-industry succession proxy: 52.3% of employer businesses have owners age 55 or older and 22% planned a five-year sale or transfer.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry
sources.supports: BLS projects aerospace products and parts real output up 2.0% annually over 2024-34, while aggregate foundry output declines 0.8% annually.

sources.title: FAA Aerospace Forecast Fiscal Years 2026-2046
sources.supports: FAA forecasts the US commercial aircraft fleet increasing from 6,949 aircraft in 2025 to 10,677 in 2046.
